content: I'm really struggling with what to do after my sabbatical. I spent 6 months traveling through South America - it was incredible, but now I'm back and my mining exploration job feels so... small.
That sounds like quite an adjustment. What was the most impactful part of your travels?

Probably seeing how different communities interact with mining operations. In Peru, I visited a town where a copper mine had both revitalized the economy and caused environmental issues. It really made me question my role in the industry.
That must have been eye-opening. How long have you been working as a geologist in mining exploration?
About 12 years now. I started right after getting my master's from Colorado School of Mines. I've worked on projects in Nevada, Arizona, and most recently in northern Canada.
That's quite a career! What made you choose geology initially?
I've always loved being outdoors and solving puzzles. Geology lets me do both - figuring out what's beneath the surface based on clues. Plus, my dad was a miner, so I grew up around it.
It sounds like it's in your blood! How has your perspective changed after seeing mining from the community side?

I'm more aware now of the social responsibility we have. I used to just focus on the technical aspects - finding deposits, assessing viability. Now I'm thinking about sustainable practices and community engagement.
That's a significant shift. Have you considered how you might incorporate these new perspectives into your work?
I've been looking into environmental consulting roles within mining companies. Maybe something that bridges the technical and social aspects. But I'm also tempted to completely switch fields.
What other fields are you considering?

I've been really into home brewing lately - made about 15 different beer recipes this year. I even won a local competition in March. I keep thinking about opening a brewpub that focuses on sustainable practices.
That's quite a leap from geology! What draws you to the brewing industry?

It combines science with creativity, which I love. Plus, there's a real movement toward sustainability in craft brewing. I could apply my geological knowledge to water conservation and sourcing local ingredients.
That's a fascinating connection! How serious are you about this brewing idea?

I'm not sure yet. I've been taking night classes on business management at the local community college. It's been helpful, but I'm still torn between staying in mining with a new focus or starting fresh.
The business classes sound like a smart move regardless of which path you choose. What's been the most valuable thing you've learned?
Probably financial planning and risk assessment. I've been working on a business plan for the brewpub - initial costs would be around $300,000. That's a scary number compared to my current salary.
That is a significant investment. Have you looked into funding options or partners?
I've talked to a few people. My brother-in-law is interested in investing - he made some money in tech and is looking for something more tangible. But I'm hesitant to mix family and business.
That's a valid concern. What are your main worries about going into business with family?
Mainly that it could strain our relationship if things go wrong. Plus, I'd feel responsible for his investment. I'd rather find other funding sources, maybe a small business loan or crowdfunding.
Those sound like prudent considerations. How are you balancing this decision-making process with your current job?

It's been challenging. I've been using my vacation time to research the brewing industry - visited over 30 breweries across South America during my sabbatical. I even took a stand-up comedy class in Buenos Aires to work on my public speaking for when I pitch the idea.
A comedy class in Buenos Aires? That's quite an adventure! How did that come about?

I've always loved listening to podcasts about comedy, and I figured if I'm going to pitch this business idea to investors, I need to be comfortable speaking in front of people. The class was terrifying but amazing - performing in Spanish was a whole other level of challenge!
That's incredibly brave! Do you think the comedy experience has changed how you approach your career decisions?

Absolutely. It taught me that it's okay to fail publicly, that preparation matters but so does adaptability. I'm trying to bring that mindset to this decision - to be bold but also realistic about the risks.
